NKJV.1: Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews.
NKJV.2: Then Paul, as his custom was, went in to them, and for three Sabbaths reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
NKJV.3: explaining and demonstrating that the Christ had to suffer and rise again from the dead, and saying, “This Jesus whom I preach to you is the Christ.”
NKJV.4: And some of them were persuaded; and a great multitude of the devout Greeks, and not a few of the leading women, joined Paul and Silas.
NKJV.5: But the Jews who were not persuaded, becoming envious, took some of the evil men from the marketplace, and gathering a mob, set all the city in an uproar and attacked the house of Jason, and sought to bring them out to the people.
NKJV.6: But when they did not find them, they dragged Jason and some brethren to the rulers of the city, crying out, “These who have turned the world upside down have come here too.
NKJV.7: Jason has harbored them, and these are all acting contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying there is another king—Jesus.”
NKJV.8: And they troubled the crowd and the rulers of the city when they heard these things.
NKJV.9: So when they had taken security from Jason and the rest, they let them go.
NKJV.10: Then the brethren immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea. When they arrived, they went into the synagogue of the Jews.
NKJV.11: These were more fair-minded than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness, and searched the Scriptures daily to find out whether these things were so.
NKJV.12: Therefore many of them believed, and also not a few of the Greeks, prominent women as well as men.
NKJV.13: But when the Jews from Thessalonica learned that the word of God was preached by Paul at Berea, they came there also and stirred up the crowds.
NKJV.14: Then immediately the brethren sent Paul away, to go to the sea; but both Silas and Timothy remained there.
NKJV.15: So those who conducted Paul brought him to Athens; and receiving a command for Silas and Timothy to come to him with all speed, they departed.
NKJV.16: Now while Paul waited for them at Athens, his spirit was provoked within him when he saw that the city was given over to idols.
NKJV.17: Therefore he reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and with the Gentile worshipers, and in the marketplace daily with those who happened to be there.
NKJV.18: Then certain Epicurean and Stoic philosophers encountered him. And some said, “What does this babbler want to say?” Others said, “He seems to be a proclaimer of foreign gods,” because he preached to them Jesus and the resurrection.
NKJV.19: And they took him and brought him to the Areopagus, saying, “May we know what this new doctrine is of which you speak?
NKJV.20: For you are bringing some strange things to our ears. Therefore we want to know what these things mean.”
NKJV.21: For all the Athenians and the foreigners who were there spent their time in nothing else but either to tell or to hear some new thing.
NKJV.22: Then Paul stood in the midst of the Areopagus and said, “Men of Athens, I perceive that in all things you are very religious;
NKJV.23: for as I was passing through and considering the objects of your worship, I even found an altar with this inscription: TO THE UNKNOWN GOD. Therefore, the One whom you worship without knowing, Him I proclaim to you:
NKJV.24: “God, who made the world and everything in it, since He is Lord of heaven and earth, does not dwell in temples made with hands.
NKJV.25: Nor is He worshiped with men’s hands, as though He needed anything, since He gives to all life, breath, and all things.
NKJV.26: And He has made from one blood every nation of men to dwell on all the face of the earth, and has determined their preappointed times and the boundaries of their dwellings,
NKJV.27: so that they should seek the Lord, in the hope that they might grope for Him and find Him, though He is not far from each one of us;
NKJV.28: for in Him we live and move and have our being, as also some of your own poets have said, ‘For we are also His offspring.’
NKJV.29: Therefore, since we are the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Divine Nature is like gold or silver or stone, something shaped by art and man’s devising.
NKJV.30: Truly, these times of ignorance God overlooked, but now commands all men everywhere to repent,
NKJV.31: because He has appointed a day on which He will judge the world in righteousness by the Man whom He has ordained. He has given assurance of this to all by raising Him from the dead.”
NKJV.32: And when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked, while others said, “We will hear you again on this matter.”
NKJV.33: So Paul departed from among them.
NKJV.34: However, some men joined him and believed, among them Dionysius the Areopagite, a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

ESV.1: Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews.
ESV.2: And Paul went in, as was his custom, and on three Sabbath days he reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
ESV.3: explaining and proving that it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and to rise from the dead, and saying, “This Jesus, whom I proclaim to you, is the Christ.”
ESV.4: And some of them were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas, as did a great many of the devout Greeks and not a few of the leading women.
ESV.5: But the Jews  were jealous, and taking some wicked men of the rabble, they formed a mob, set the city in an uproar, and attacked the house of Jason, seeking to bring them out to the crowd.
ESV.6: And when they could not find them, they dragged Jason and some of the brothers before the city authorities, shouting, “These men who have turned the world upside down have come here also,
ESV.7: and Jason has received them, and they are all acting against the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, Jesus.”
ESV.8: And the people and the city authorities were disturbed when they heard these things.
ESV.9: And when they had taken money as security from Jason and the rest, they let them go.
ESV.10: The brothers  immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea, and when they arrived they went into the Jewish synagogue.
ESV.11: Now these Jews were more noble than those in Thessalonica; they received the word with all eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily to see if these things were so.
ESV.12: Many of them therefore believed, with not a few Greek women of high standing as well as men.
ESV.13: But when the Jews from Thessalonica learned that the word of God was proclaimed by Paul at Berea also, they came there too, agitating and stirring up the crowds.
ESV.14: Then the brothers immediately sent Paul off on his way to the sea, but Silas and Timothy remained there.
ESV.15: Those who conducted Paul brought him as far as Athens, and after receiving a command for Silas and Timothy to come to him as soon as possible, they departed.
ESV.16: Now while Paul was waiting for them at Athens, his spirit was provoked within him as he saw that the city was full of idols.
ESV.17: So he reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and the devout persons, and in the marketplace every day with those who happened to be there.
ESV.18: Some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers also conversed with him. And some said, “What does this babbler wish to say?” Others said, “He seems to be a preacher of foreign divinities” — because he was preaching Jesus and the resurrection.
ESV.19: And they took him and brought him to the Areopagus, saying, “May we know what this new teaching is that you are presenting?
ESV.20: For you bring some strange things to our ears. We wish to know therefore what these things mean.”
ESV.21: Now all the Athenians and the foreigners who lived there would spend their time in nothing except telling or hearing something new.
ESV.22: So Paul, standing in the midst of the Areopagus, said: “Men of Athens, I perceive that in every way you are very religious.
ESV.23: For as I passed along and observed the objects of your worship, I found also an altar with this inscription: ‘To the unknown god.’ What therefore you worship as unknown, this I proclaim to you.
ESV.24: The God who made the world and everything in it, being Lord of heaven and earth, does not live in temples made by man,
ESV.25: nor is he served by human hands, as though he needed anything, since he himself gives to all mankind life and breath and everything.
ESV.26: And he made from one man every nation of mankind to live on all the face of the earth, having determined allotted periods and the boundaries of their dwelling place,
ESV.27: that they should seek God, and perhaps feel their way toward him and find him. Yet he is actually not far from each one of us,
ESV.28: for “‘In him we live and move and have our being’;   as even some of your own poets have said, “‘For we are indeed his offspring.’
ESV.29: Being then God's offspring, we ought not to think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and imagination of man.
ESV.30: The times of ignorance God overlooked, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent,
ESV.31: because he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed; and of this he has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead.”
ESV.32: Now when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked. But others said, “We will hear you again about this.”
ESV.33: So Paul went out from their midst.
ESV.34: But some men joined him and believed, among whom also were Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris and others with them.

NASB.1: Now when they had traveled through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews.
NASB.2: And according to Paul's custom, he went to them, and for three Sabbaths reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
NASB.3: explaining and giving evidence that the Christ had to suffer and rise again from the dead, and [saying], 'This Jesus whom I am proclaiming to you is the Christ.'
NASB.4: And some of them were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas, along with a large number of the God-fearing Greeks and a number of the leading women.
NASB.5: But the Jews, becoming jealous and taking along some wicked men from the market place, formed a mob and set the city in an uproar; and attacking the house of Jason, they were seeking to bring them out to the people.
NASB.6: When they did not find them, they [began] dragging Jason and some brethren before the city authorities, shouting, 'These men who have upset the world have come here also;
NASB.7: and Jason has welcomed them, and they all act contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, Jesus.'
NASB.8: They stirred up the crowd and the city authorities who heard these things.
NASB.9: And when they had received a pledge from Jason and the others, they released them.
NASB.10: The brethren immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea, and when they arrived, they went into the synagogue of the Jews.
NASB.11: Now these were more noble-minded than those in Thessalonica, for they received the word with great eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily [to see] whether these things were so.
NASB.12: Therefore many of them believed, along with a number of prominent Greek women and men.
NASB.13: But when the Jews of Thessalonica found out that the word of God had been proclaimed by Paul in Berea also, they came there as well, agitating and stirring up the crowds.
NASB.14: Then immediately the brethren sent Paul out to go as far as the sea; and Silas and Timothy remained there.
NASB.15: Now those who escorted Paul brought him as far as Athens; and receiving a command for Silas and Timothy to come to him as soon as possible, they left.
NASB.16: Now while Paul was waiting for them at Athens, his spirit was being provoked within him as he was observing the city full of idols.
NASB.17: So he was reasoning in the synagogue with the Jews and the God-fearing [Gentiles], and in the market place every day with those who happened to be present.
NASB.18: And also some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers were conversing with him. Some were saying, 'What would this idle babbler wish to say?' Others, 'He seems to be a proclaimer of strange deities,'-- because he was preaching Jesus and the resurrection.
NASB.19: And they took him and brought him to the Areopagus, saying, 'May we know what this new teaching is which you are proclaiming?
NASB.20: 'For you are bringing some strange things to our ears; so we want to know what these things mean.'
NASB.21: (Now all the Athenians and the strangers visiting there used to spend their time in nothing other than telling or hearing something new.)
NASB.22: So Paul stood in the midst of the Areopagus and said, 'Men of Athens, I observe that you are very religious in all respects.
NASB.23: 'For while I was passing through and examining the objects of your worship, I also found an altar with this inscription, 'TO AN UNKNOWN GOD.' Therefore what you worship in ignorance, this I proclaim to you.
NASB.24: 'The God who made the world and all things in it, since He is Lord of heaven and earth, does not dwell in temples made with hands;
NASB.25: nor is He served by human hands, as though He needed anything, since He Himself gives to all [people] life and breath and all things;
NASB.26: and He made from one [man] every nation of mankind to live on all the face of the earth, having determined [their] appointed times and the boundaries of their habitation,
NASB.27: that they would seek God, if perhaps they might grope for Him and find Him, though He is not far from each one of us;
NASB.28: for in Him we live and move and exist, as even some of your own poets have said, 'For we also are His children.'
NASB.29: 'Being then the children of God, we ought not to think that the Divine Nature is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and thought of man.
NASB.30: 'Therefore having overlooked the times of ignorance, God is now declaring to men that all [people] everywhere should repent,
NASB.31: because He has fixed a day in which He will judge the world in righteousness through a Man whom He has appointed, having furnished proof to all men by raising Him from the dead.'
NASB.32: Now when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some [began] to sneer, but others said, 'We shall hear you again concerning this.'
NASB.33: So Paul went out of their midst.
NASB.34: But some men joined him and believed, among whom also were Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris and others with them.

NLT.1: Paul and Silas then traveled through the towns of Amphipolis and Apollonia and came to Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue.
NLT.2: As was Paul’s custom, he went to the synagogue service, and for three Sabbaths in a row he used the Scriptures to reason with the people.
NLT.3: He explained the prophecies and proved that the Messiah must suffer and rise from the dead. He said, “This Jesus I’m telling you about is the Messiah.”
NLT.4: Some of the Jews who listened were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas, along with many God-fearing Greek men and quite a few prominent women.
NLT.5: But some of the Jews were jealous, so they gathered some troublemakers from the marketplace to form a mob and start a riot. They attacked the home of Jason, searching for Paul and Silas so they could drag them out to the crowd.
NLT.6: Not finding them there, they dragged out Jason and some of the other believers instead and took them before the city council. “Paul and Silas have caused trouble all over the world,” they shouted, “and now they are here disturbing our city, too.
NLT.7: And Jason has welcomed them into his home. They are all guilty of treason against Caesar, for they profess allegiance to another king, named Jesus.”
NLT.8: The people of the city, as well as the city council, were thrown into turmoil by these reports.
NLT.9: So the officials forced Jason and the other believers to post bond, and then they released them.
NLT.10: That very night the believers sent Paul and Silas to Berea. When they arrived there, they went to the Jewish synagogue.
NLT.11: And the people of Berea were more open-minded than those in Thessalonica, and they listened eagerly to Paul’s message. They searched the Scriptures day after day to see if Paul and Silas were teaching the truth.
NLT.12: As a result, many Jews believed, as did many of the prominent Greek women and men.
NLT.13: But when some Jews in Thessalonica learned that Paul was preaching the word of God in Berea, they went there and stirred up trouble.
NLT.14: The believers acted at once, sending Paul on to the coast, while Silas and Timothy remained behind.
NLT.15: Those escorting Paul went with him all the way to Athens; then they returned to Berea with instructions for Silas and Timothy to hurry and join him.
NLT.16: While Paul was waiting for them in Athens, he was deeply troubled by all the idols he saw everywhere in the city.
NLT.17: He went to the synagogue to reason with the Jews and the God-fearing Gentiles, and he spoke daily in the public square to all who happened to be there.
NLT.18: He also had a debate with some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers. When he told them about Jesus and his resurrection, they said, “What’s this babbler trying to say with these strange ideas he’s picked up?” Others said, “He seems to be preaching about some foreign gods.”
NLT.19: Then they took him to the high council of the city. “Come and tell us about this new teaching,” they said.
NLT.20: “You are saying some rather strange things, and we want to know what it’s all about.”
NLT.21: (It should be explained that all the Athenians as well as the foreigners in Athens seemed to spend all their time discussing the latest ideas.)
NLT.22: So Paul, standing before the council, addressed them as follows: “Men of Athens, I notice that you are very religious in every way,
NLT.23: for as I was walking along I saw your many shrines. And one of your altars had this inscription on it: ‘To an Unknown God.’ This God, whom you worship without knowing, is the one I’m telling you about.
NLT.24: “He is the God who made the world and everything in it. Since he is Lord of heaven and earth, he doesn’t live in man-made temples,
NLT.25: and human hands can’t serve his needs — for he has no needs. He himself gives life and breath to everything, and he satisfies every need.
NLT.26: From one man he created all the nations throughout the whole earth. He decided beforehand when they should rise and fall, and he determined their boundaries.
NLT.27: “His purpose was for the nations to seek after God and perhaps feel their way toward him and find him — though he is not far from any one of us.
NLT.28: For in him we live and move and exist. As some of your own poets have said, ‘We are his offspring.’
NLT.29: And since this is true, we shouldn’t think of God as an idol designed by craftsmen from gold or silver or stone.
NLT.30: “God overlooked people’s ignorance about these things in earlier times, but now he commands everyone everywhere to repent of their sins and turn to him.
NLT.31: For he has set a day for judging the world with justice by the man he has appointed, and he proved to everyone who this is by raising him from the dead.”
NLT.32: When they heard Paul speak about the resurrection of the dead, some laughed in contempt, but others said, “We want to hear more about this later.”
NLT.33: That ended Paul’s discussion with them,
NLT.34: but some joined him and became believers. Among them were Dionysius, a member of the council, a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

WEB.1: Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue.
WEB.2: Paul, as was his custom, went in to them, and for three Sabbath days reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
WEB.3: explaining and demonstrating that the Christ had to suffer and rise again from the dead, and saying, “This Jesus, whom I proclaim to you, is the Christ.”
WEB.4: Some of them were persuaded, and joined Paul and Silas, of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and not a few of the chief women.
WEB.5: But the unpersuaded Jews took along  some wicked men from the marketplace, and gathering a crowd, set the city in an uproar. Assaulting the house of Jason, they sought to bring them out to the people.
WEB.6: When they didn’t find them, they dragged Jason and certain brothers  before the rulers of the city, crying, “These who have turned the world upside down have come here also,
WEB.7: whom Jason has received. These all act contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, Jesus!”
WEB.8: The multitude and the rulers of the city were troubled when they heard these things.
WEB.9: When they had taken security from Jason and the rest, they let them go.
WEB.10: The brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Beroea. When they arrived, they went into the Jewish synagogue.
WEB.11: Now these were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness of mind, examining the Scriptures daily to see whether these things were so.
WEB.12: Many of them therefore believed; also of the prominent Greek women, and not a few men.
WEB.13: But when the Jews of Thessalonica had knowledge that the word of God was proclaimed by Paul at Beroea also, they came there likewise, agitating the multitudes.
WEB.14: Then the brothers immediately sent out Paul to go as far as to the sea, and Silas and Timothy still stayed there.
WEB.15: But those who escorted Paul brought him as far as Athens. Receiving a commandment to Silas and Timothy that they should come to him very quickly, they departed.
WEB.16: Now while Paul waited for them at Athens, his spirit was provoked within him as he saw the city full of idols.
WEB.17: So he reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and the devout persons, and in the marketplace every day with those who met him.
WEB.18: Some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers also  were conversing with him. Some said, “What does this babbler want to say?” Others said, “He seems to be advocating foreign deities,” because he preached Jesus and the resurrection.
WEB.19: They took hold of him, and brought him to the Areopagus, saying, “May we know what this new teaching is, which is spoken by you?
WEB.20: For you bring certain strange things to our ears. We want to know therefore what these things mean.”
WEB.21: Now all the Athenians and the strangers living there spent their time in nothing else, but either to tell or to hear some new thing.
WEB.22: Paul stood in the middle of the Areopagus, and said, “You men of Athens, I perceive that you are very religious in all things.
WEB.23: For as I passed along, and observed the objects of your worship, I found also an altar with this inscription: ‘TO AN UNKNOWN GOD.’ What therefore you worship in ignorance, this I announce to you.
WEB.24: The God who made the world and all things in it, he, being Lord of heaven and earth, doesn’t dwell in temples made with hands,
WEB.25: neither is he served by men’s hands, as though he needed anything, seeing he himself gives to all life and breath, and all things.
WEB.26: He made from one blood every nation of men to dwell on all the surface of the earth, having determined appointed seasons, and the boundaries of their dwellings,
WEB.27: that they should seek the Lord, if perhaps they might reach out for him and find him, though he is not far from each one of us.
WEB.28: ‘For in him we live, and move, and have our being.’ As some of your own poets have said, ‘For we are also his offspring.’
WEB.29: Being then the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Divine Nature is like gold, or silver, or stone, engraved by art and design of man.
WEB.30: The times of ignorance therefore God overlooked. But now he commands that all people everywhere should repent,
WEB.31: because he has appointed a day in which he will judge the world in righteousness by the man whom he has ordained; of which he has given assurance to all men, in that he has raised him from the dead.”
WEB.32: Now when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked; but others said, “We want to hear you again concerning this.”
WEB.33: Thus Paul went out from among them.
WEB.34: But certain men joined with him, and believed, among whom also was Dionysius the Areopagite, and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

CSB17.1: After they passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, <sup>ⓑ</sup> where there was a Jewish synagogue.
CSB17.2: As usual, Paul went into the synagogue, and on three Sabbath days reasoned with them from the Scriptures, <sup>ⓒ</sup>
CSB17.3: explaining and proving that it was necessary for the Messiah to suffer and rise from the dead: “This Jesus I am proclaiming to you is the Messiah.” <sup>ⓓ</sup>
CSB17.4: Some of them were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas, including a large number of God-fearing Greeks, as well as a number of the leading women. <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.5: But the Jews became jealous, and they brought together some wicked men from the marketplace, formed a mob, and started a riot in the city. Attacking Jason’s house, they searched for them to bring them out to the public assembly. <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.6: When they did not find them, they dragged Jason and some of the brothers before the city officials, shouting, “These men who have turned the world upside down have come here too,
CSB17.7: and Jason has welcomed them. They are all acting contrary to Caesar’s decrees, <sup>ⓖ</sup> saying that there is another king — Jesus.” <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.8: The crowd and city officials who heard these things were upset.
CSB17.9: After taking a security bond from Jason and the others, they released them.
CSB17.10: As soon as it was night, the brothers and sisters sent Paul and Silas away to Berea. <sup>ⓘ</sup> Upon arrival, they went into the synagogue of the Jews.
CSB17.11: The people here were of more noble character than those in Thessalonica, since they received the word with eagerness and examined the Scriptures <sup>ⓙ</sup> daily to see if these things were so.
CSB17.12: Consequently, many of them believed, including a number of the prominent Greek women as well as men.
CSB17.13: But when the Jews from Thessalonica found out that the word of God had been proclaimed by Paul at Berea, they came there too, agitating and upsetting <sup>[1]</sup> the crowds.
CSB17.14: Then the brothers and sisters immediately sent Paul away to go to the coast, but Silas and Timothy <sup>ⓚ</sup> stayed on there.
CSB17.15: Those who escorted Paul brought him as far as Athens, and after receiving instructions for Silas and Timothy to come to him as quickly as possible, they departed. <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.16: While Paul was waiting for them in Athens, he was deeply distressed when he saw that the city was full of idols.
CSB17.17: So he reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and with those who worshiped God, as well as in the marketplace every day with those who happened to be there. <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.18: Some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers also debated with him. Some said, “What is this ignorant show-off <sup>[2]</sup> trying to say?” Others replied, “He seems to be a preacher of foreign deities” — because he was telling the good news about Jesus and the resurrection. <sup>ⓝ</sup>
CSB17.19: They took him and brought him to the Areopagus, <sup>[3]</sup> and said, “May we learn about this new teaching you are presenting? <sup>ⓐ</sup>
CSB17.20: Because what you say sounds strange to us, and we want to know what these things mean.”
CSB17.21: Now all the Athenians and the foreigners residing there spent their time on nothing else but telling or hearing something new.
CSB17.22: Paul stood in the middle of the Areopagus and said: “People of Athens! I see that you are extremely religious in every respect.
CSB17.23: For as I was passing through and observing the objects of your worship, I even found an altar on which was inscribed: ‘To an Unknown God.’ Therefore, what you worship in ignorance, this I proclaim to you.
CSB17.24: The God who made the world and everything in it <sup>ⓑ</sup>  — he is Lord of heaven and earth <sup>ⓒ</sup>  — does not live in shrines made by hands. <sup>ⓓ</sup>
CSB17.25: Neither is he served by human hands, as though he needed anything, <sup>ⓔ</sup> since he himself gives everyone life and breath and all things. <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.26: From one man <sup>[4]</sup> he has made every nationality to live over the whole earth and has determined their appointed times and the boundaries of where they live. <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.27: He did this so that they might seek God, and perhaps they might reach out and find him, though he is not far from each one of us. <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.28: For in him we live and move and have our being, as even some of your own poets have said, ‘For we are also his offspring.’ <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.29: Since we are God’s offspring then, we shouldn’t think that the divine nature is like gold or silver or stone, an image fashioned by human art and imagination. <sup>ⓙ</sup>
CSB17.30: “Therefore, having overlooked <sup>ⓚ</sup> the times of ignorance, God now commands all people everywhere to repent,
CSB17.31: because he has set a day when he is going to judge the world in righteousness by the man he has appointed. He has provided proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead.” <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.32: When they heard about the resurrection of the dead, some began to ridicule him, but others said, “We’d like to hear from you again about this.”
CSB17.34: However, some people joined him and believed, including Dionysius the Areopagite, a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

AMP.1: Now after Paul and Silas had traveled through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews.
AMP.2: And Paul entered the synagogue, as was his custom, and for three Sabbaths he engaged in discussion and friendly debate with them from the Scriptures,
AMP.3: explaining and pointing out [scriptural evidence] that it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and rise from the dead, and saying, "This Jesus, whom I am proclaiming to you, is the Christ (the Messiah, the Anointed)."
AMP.4: And some of them were persuaded to believe and joined Paul and Silas, along with a large number of the God-fearing Greeks and many of the leading women.
AMP.5: But the [unbelieving] Jews became jealous, and taking along some thugs from [the lowlifes in] the market place, they formed a mob and set the city in an uproar; and then attacking Jason's house tried to bring Paul and Silas out to the people.
AMP.6: But when they failed to find them, they dragged Jason and some brothers before the city authorities, shouting, "These men who have turned the world upside down have come here too;
AMP.7: and Jason has welcomed them [into his house and protected them]! And they all are saying things contrary to the decrees of Caesar, [actually] claiming that there is another king, Jesus."
AMP.8: They stirred up the crowd and the city authorities who heard these things.
AMP.9: And when they had taken security (bail) from Jason and the others, they let them go.
AMP.10: The brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea; and when they arrived, they entered the Jewish synagogue.
AMP.11: Now these people were more noble and open-minded than those in Thessalonica, so they received the message [of salvation through faith in the Christ] with great eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily to see if these things were so.
AMP.12: As a result many of them became believers, together with a number of prominent Greek women and men.
AMP.13: But when the Jews of Thessalonica learned that the word of God [concerning eternal salvation through faith in Christ] had also been preached by Paul at Berea, they came there too, agitating and disturbing the crowds.
AMP.14: So at that time the brothers immediately sent Paul away to go as far as the sea; but Silas and Timothy remained there [at Berea].
AMP.15: Those who escorted Paul brought him to Athens; and [after] receiving instructions [from Paul] for Silas and Timothy to join him as soon as possible, they left.
AMP.16: Now while Paul was waiting for them at Athens, his spirit was greatly angered when he saw that the city was full of idols.
AMP.17: So he had discussions in the synagogue with the Jews and the God-fearing Gentiles, and in the market place day after day with any who happened to be there.
AMP.18: And some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers began to engage in conversation with him. And some said, "What could this idle babbler [with his eclectic, scrap-heap learning] have in mind to say?" Others said, "He seems to be a proclaimer of strange deities"—because he was preaching the good news about Jesus and the resurrection.
AMP.19: They took him and brought him to the Areopagus (Hill of Ares, the Greek god of war), saying, "May we know what this [strange] new teaching is which you are proclaiming?
AMP.20: For you are bringing some startling and strange things to our ears; so we want to know what they mean."
AMP.21: (Now all the Athenians and the foreigners visiting there used to spend their [leisure] time in nothing other than telling or hearing something new.)
AMP.22: So Paul, standing in the center of the Areopagus, said: "Men of Athens, I observe [with every turn I make throughout the city] that you are very religious and devout in all respects.
AMP.23: Now as I was going along and carefully looking at your objects of worship, I came to an altar with this inscription: 'TO AN UNKNOWN GOD.' Therefore what you already worship as unknown, this I proclaim to you.
AMP.24: The God who created the world and everything in it, since He is Lord of heaven and earth, does not dwell in temples made with hands;
AMP.25: nor is He served by human hands, as though He needed anything, because it is He who gives to all [people] life and breath and all things.
AMP.26: And He made from one man every nation of mankind to live on the face of the earth, having determined their appointed times and the boundaries of their lands and territories.
AMP.27: This was so that they would seek God, if perhaps they might grasp for Him and find Him, though He is not far from each one of us.
AMP.28: For in Him we live and move and exist [that is, in Him we actually have our being], as even some of your own poets have said, 'For we also are His children.'
AMP.29: So then, being God's children, we should not think that the Divine Nature (deity) is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and imagination or skill of man.
AMP.30: Therefore God overlooked and disregarded the former ages of ignorance; but now He commands all people everywhere to repent [that is, to change their old way of thinking, to regret their past sins, and to seek God's purpose for their lives],
AMP.31: because He has set a day when He will judge the inhabited world in righteousness by a Man whom He has appointed and destined for that task, and He has provided credible proof to everyone by raising Him from the dead."
AMP.32: Now when they heard [the term] resurrection from the dead, some mocked and sneered; but others said, "We will hear from you again about this matter."
AMP.34: But some men joined him and believed; among them were Dionysius, [a judge] of the Council of Areopagus, and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

MSG.1: They took the road south through Amphipolis and Apollonia to Thessalonica, where there was a community of Jews.
MSG.2: Paul went to their meeting place, as he usually did when he came!to a town, and for three Sabbaths running he preached to them from the Scriptures.
MSG.3: He opened up the texts so they understood what they'd been reading all their lives: that the Messiah absolutely had to be put to death and raised from the dead—there were no other options—and that "this Jesus I'm introducing you to is that Messiah."
MSG.4: Some of them were won over and joined ranks with Paul and Silas, among them a great many God-fearing Greeks and a considerable number of women from the aristocracy.
MSG.5: But the hard-line Jews became furious over the conversions. Mad with jealousy, they rounded up a bunch of brawlers off the streets and soon had an ugly mob terrorizing the city as they hunted down Paul and Silas. They broke into Jason's house, thinking that Paul and Silas were there.
MSG.6: When they couldn't find them, they collared Jason and his friends instead and dragged them before the city fathers, yelling hysterically, "These people are out to destroy the world, and now they've shown up on our doorstep, attacking everything we hold dear!
MSG.7: And Jason is hiding them, these traitors and turncoats who say Jesus is king and Caesar is nothing!"
MSG.8: The city fathers and the crowd of people were totally alarmed by what they heard.
MSG.9: They made Jason and his friends post heavy bail and let them go while they investigated the charges.
MSG.10: That night, under cover of darkness, their friends got Paul and Silas out of town as fast as they could. They sent them to Berea, where they again met with the Jewish community.
MSG.11: They were treated a lot better there than in Thessalonica. The Jews received Paul's message with enthusiasm and met with him daily, examining the Scriptures to see if they supported what he said.
MSG.12: A lot of them became believers, including many Greeks who were prominent in the community, women and men of influence.
MSG.13: But it wasn't long before reports got back to the Thessalonian hard-line Jews that Paul was at it again, preaching the Word of God, this time in Berea. They lost no time responding, and created a mob scene there, too.
MSG.14: With the help of his friends, Paul gave them the slip—caught a boat and put out to sea. Silas and Timothy stayed behind.
MSG.15: The men who helped Paul escape got him as far as Athens and left him there. Paul sent word back with them to Silas and Timothy: "Come as quickly as you can!"
MSG.16: The longer Paul waited in Athens for Silas and Timothy, the angrier he got—all those idols! The city was a junkyard of idols.
MSG.17: He discussed it with the Jews and other like-minded people at their meeting place. And every day he went out on the streets and talked with anyone who happened along.
MSG.18: He got to know some of the Epicurean and Stoic intellectuals pretty well through these conversations. Some of them dismissed him with sarcasm: "What an airhead!" But others, listening to him go on about Jesus and the resurrection, were intrigued: "That's a new slant on the gods. Tell us more."
MSG.19: These people got together and asked him to make a public presentation over at the Areopagus, where things were a little quieter. They said, "This is a new one on us. We've never heard anything quite like it. Where did you come up with this anyway?
MSG.20: Explain it so we can understand."
MSG.21: Downtown Athens was a great place for gossip. There were always people hanging around, natives and tourists alike, waiting for the latest tidbit on most anything.
MSG.22: So Paul took his stand in the open space at the Areopagus and laid it out for them. "It is plain to see that you Athenians take your religion seriously.
MSG.23: When I arrived here the other day, I was fascinated with all the shrines I came across. And then I found one inscribed, to the god nobody knows. I'm here to introduce you to this God so you can worship intelligently, know who you're dealing with.
MSG.24: "The God who made the world and everything in it, this Master of sky and land, doesn't live in custom-made shrines
MSG.25: or need the human race to run errands for him, as if he couldn't take care of himself. He makes the creatures; the creatures don't make him.
MSG.26: Starting from scratch, he made the entire human race and made the earth hospitable, with plenty of time and space for living
MSG.27: so we could seek after God, and not just grope around in the dark but actually find him. He doesn't play hide-and-seek with us. He's not remote; he's near.
MSG.28: We live and move in him, can't get away from him! One of your poets said it well: 'We're the God-created.'
MSG.29: Well, if we are the God-created, it doesn't make a lot of sense to think we could hire a sculptor to chisel a god out of stone for us, does it?
MSG.30: "God overlooks it as long as you don't know any better—but that time is past. The unknown is now known, and he's calling for a radical life-change.
MSG.31: He has set a day when the entire human race will be judged and everything set right. And he has already appointed the judge, confirming him before everyone by raising him from the dead."
MSG.32: At the phrase "raising him from the dead," the listeners split: Some laughed at him and walked off making jokes; others said, "Let's do this again. We want to hear more."
MSG.33: But that was it for the day, and Paul left.
MSG.34: There were still others, it turned out, who were convinced then and there, and stuck with Paul—among them Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris.

NIV.1:
In Thessalonica
When they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue.
NIV.2: As his custom was, Paul went into the synagogue, and on three Sabbath days he reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
NIV.3: explaining and proving that the Christ had to suffer and rise from the dead. “This Jesus I am proclaiming to you is the Christ,” he said.
NIV.4: Some of the Jews were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas, as did a large number of God-fearing Greeks and not a few prominent women.
NIV.5: But the Jews were jealous; so they rounded up some bad characters from the marketplace, formed a mob and started a riot in the city. They rushed to Jason’s house in search of Paul and Silas in order to bring them out to the crowd.
NIV.6: But when they did not find them, they dragged Jason and some other brothers before the city officials, shouting: “These men who have caused trouble all over the world have now come here,
NIV.7: and Jason has welcomed them into his house. They are all defying Caesar’s decrees, saying that there is another king, one called Jesus.”
NIV.8: When they heard this, the crowd and the city officials were thrown into turmoil.
NIV.9: Then they made Jason and the others post bond and let them go.
NIV.10:
In Berea
As soon as it was night, the brothers sent Paul and Silas away to Berea. On arriving there, they went to the Jewish synagogue.
NIV.11: Now the Bereans were of more noble character than the Thessalonians, for they received the message with great eagerness and examined the Scriptures every day to see if what Paul said was true.
NIV.12: Many of the Jews believed, as did also a number of prominent Greek women and many Greek men.
NIV.13: When the Jews in Thessalonica learned that Paul was preaching the word of God at Berea, they went there too, agitating the crowds and stirring them up.
NIV.14: The brothers immediately sent Paul to the coast, but Silas and Timothy stayed at Berea.
NIV.15: The men who escorted Paul brought him to Athens and then left with instructions for Silas and Timothy to join him as soon as possible.
NIV.16:
In Athens
While Paul was waiting for them in Athens, he was greatly distressed to see that the city was full of idols.
NIV.17: So he reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and the God-fearing Greeks, as well as in the marketplace day by day with those who happened to be there.
NIV.18: A group of Epicurean and Stoic philosophers began to dispute with him. Some of them asked, “What is this babbler trying to say?” Others remarked, “He seems to be advocating foreign gods.” They said this because Paul was preaching the good news about Jesus and the resurrection.
NIV.19: Then they took him and brought him to a meeting of the Areopagus, where they said to him, “May we know what this new teaching is that you are presenting?
NIV.20: You are bringing some strange ideas to our ears, and we want to know what they mean.”
NIV.21: (All the Athenians and the foreigners who lived there spent their time doing nothing but talking about and listening to the latest ideas.)
NIV.22: Paul then stood up in the meeting of the Areopagus and said: “Men of Athens! I see that in every way you are very religious.
NIV.23: For as I walked around and looked carefully at your objects of worship, I even found an altar with this inscription: to an unknown god. Now what you worship as something unknown I am going to proclaim to you.
NIV.24: “The God who made the world and everything in it is the Lord of heaven and earth and does not live in temples built by hands.
NIV.25: And he is not served by human hands, as if he needed anything, because he himself gives all men life and breath and everything else.
NIV.26: From one man he made every nation of men, that they should inhabit the whole earth; and he determined the times set for them and the exact places where they should live.
NIV.27: God did this so that men would seek him and perhaps reach out for him and find him, though he is not far from each one of us.
NIV.28: ‘For in him we live and move and have our being.’ As some of your own poets have said, ‘We are his offspring.’
NIV.29: “Therefore since we are God’s offspring, we should not think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone—an image made by man’s design and skill.
NIV.30: In the past God overlooked such ignorance, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent.
NIV.31: For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to all men by raising him from the dead.”
NIV.32: When they heard about the resurrection of the dead, some of them sneered, but others said, “We want to hear you again on this subject.”
NIV.33: At that, Paul left the Council.
NIV.34: A few men became followers of Paul and believed. Among them was Dionysius, a member of the Areopagus, also a woman named Damaris, and a number of others.
